sentences.grammarNote: 예약 (yeyak) = reservation/booking — Sino-Korean: 예 (豫, in advance) + 약 (約, promise). 이 = subject marker after a consonant: 예약이 있어요 (a reservation exists = I have a reservation). 있다 handles both existence and possession — there is no separate verb for 'have' in Korean.

체크인 (chekeuin) = check-in — English loanword. Adding 하다 to a loanword noun creates a verb: 체크인하다 (to check in), 체크아웃하다 (to check out). -고 싶어요 = 'would like to / want to' — one of the most polite and useful request expressions.
실 (sil) means 'room'. 1인실 (il insil) = single room, 2인실 (i insil) = double room. The Sino-Korean number prefix + 인 (person) + 실 (room) pattern applies throughout Korean accommodation vocabulary.

sentences.base_language: What floor is the room on?
sentences.grammarNote: 층 (cheung) = floor/story. Ground floor is 1층 (il cheung). 몇 (myeot) = 'how many / what number', used before counters: 몇 층 (what floor), 몇 시 (what time), 몇 명 (how many people).

오 (o) = five — Sino-Korean number used for floors. Korean uses Sino-Korean numbers (일, 이, 삼, 사, 오...) for floor numbers, room numbers, and measurements. 이에요 = copula after a consonant: 오층이에요. The ground floor is 1층 (il cheung) — Korea uses the same system as Europe, not the British 'ground floor'.

비밀번호 (bimilbeonho) = password — Sino-Korean: 비밀 (秘密, secret) + 번호 (番號, number). 가 = subject marker after a vowel: 비밀번호가. 뭐예요? = 'what is it?' — 뭐 (what) + 이에요 contracted to 예요 after a vowel. This is one of the most practical hotel/café phrases.

sentences.base_language: I'll check out early tomorrow morning.
sentences.grammarNote: -(으)ㄹ게요 expresses the speaker's decision or intention: 체크아웃할게요 = 'I will check out'. 일찍 (iljjik) = early — a useful adverb, no particle needed. 내일 아침 (naeil achim) = tomorrow morning — time expressions are stacked from large to small: tomorrow → morning.

잘 자요 (jal jayo) = 'good night' (literally 'sleep well'). More intimate: 잘 자 (jal ja). The formal 안녕히 주무세요 (annyeonghi jumuseyo) uses an entirely different, honorific verb for sleeping — 주무시다 — reserved for elders and superiors.
